description: Music-theory grounding for the music/studio lens — the harmonic, rhythmic, and acoustic principles behind composition and production.

entries.title: Intervals & the harmonic series
entries.summary: An interval is the distance between two pitches; consonance arises from simple frequency ratios (octave 2:1, fifth 3:2, fourth 4:3) found in the natural harmonic series of any vibrating string or column. This physics is why those intervals sound stable across cultures and why the overtone content of a sound defines its timbre.

entries.title: Scales, keys & modes
entries.summary: A scale is an ordered set of pitches; the major scale's W-W-H-W-W-W-H step pattern (and its relative minor) anchors Western tonality. The seven modes (Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian…) rotate that pattern to different starting degrees, each with a characteristic color (Dorian's raised 6th, Phrygian's flat 2nd) used to set mood without leaving the parent key.

entries.title: Chords & functional harmony
entries.summary: Chords stack thirds (triads: root-3rd-5th; sevenths add tension). In functional harmony chords have roles — tonic (I, home), subdominant (IV, departure), dominant (V, tension demanding resolution) — and a cadence like V→I creates the sense of arrival. The ii–V–I is the gravitational center of tonal and jazz harmony.

entries.title: Rhythm, meter & groove
entries.summary: Meter groups beats (4/4, 3/4, 6/8); tempo is their speed (BPM). Groove comes from where notes sit relative to the grid — syncopation accents off-beats, and micro-timing (swing, push/pull) is what makes a performance feel human versus quantized. Polyrhythm layers conflicting groupings (3-against-2) for tension.

entries.title: Voice leading & counterpoint
entries.summary: Voice leading moves each melodic line smoothly between chords (small steps, resolving tendency tones, avoiding parallel fifths/octaves in strict counterpoint). Good voice leading is why a progression sounds connected rather than like disconnected block chords — the craft beneath both Bach chorales and pad arrangements.

entries.title: EQ, frequency masking & the mix
entries.summary: Mixing balances elements across the frequency spectrum; masking occurs when sounds compete in the same band (kick vs bass around 60–120 Hz), muddying the mix. EQ carves space — high-pass to clear low-end clutter, complementary cuts/boosts so each element occupies its own range. Clarity is won by subtraction more than boosting.

entries.title: Dynamic range & compression
entries.summary: A compressor reduces dynamic range by attenuating signal above a threshold (ratio, attack, release set how and how fast), then make-up gain raises the whole. It controls peaks, adds sustain/punch, and glues a mix; over-compression kills life (the loudness war). Attack/release timing relative to the groove is what makes it musical.

entries.title: Synthesis & the ADSR envelope
entries.summary: Subtractive synthesis starts with harmonically-rich oscillators (saw/square), shapes them with a filter (cutoff/resonance), and controls amplitude over time with an ADSR envelope — Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release. Short attack + short release = plucky; slow attack = pads. The same envelope concept modulates filter and pitch for movement.

entries.title: Tension, release & song form
entries.summary: Music holds attention by building and releasing tension — harmonic (dominant→tonic), dynamic (quiet→loud), textural (sparse→dense), and registral. Song forms (verse-chorus, AABA, the EDM build-drop) are conventions for pacing that arc; an arrangement that adds/removes elements over time is managing the listener's expectation curve.

entries.title: Tuning & equal temperament
entries.summary: Pure (just) intervals from the harmonic series can't all be kept while playing in every key, so 12-tone equal temperament divides the octave into 12 equal ratios (2^(1/12)), making every key equally (slightly) out-of-tune but modulation seamless. It's the compromise that underlies the piano and nearly all Western instruments — a deliberate, useful imperfection.
